preamble: The shift from a retroflex stop [ʈ] to an alveolar stop [t] is a form of de-retroflexion involving apical displacement—moving from postvocalic to prevocalic position while fronting from the hard palate to the alveolar ridge. This process is well-documented in South Dravidian languages (Telugu, Kannada) where Proto-Dravidian retroflex stops underwent systematic repositioning and deretroflexion, and is a common phonological simplification in languages with marginal retroflex series.

examples.note: Major apical displacement: retroflex stops shifted from postvocalic to prevocalic position, often deretroflexing to alveolar [t] or voiced [d]. Proto-Dravidian *uẓ-u 'plow' → Telugu ḍu-kki; *car-a-cu → Telugu trācu.
Kannada merged the three-way coronal distinction into two-way dental-retroflex, with many retroflex stops dentalizing to [t].

examples.to: Tamil /ṭ/ (largely preserved, but some dialects show [t] variants)
examples.note: Unlike Kannada/Telugu, Tamil preserved the retroflex-dental distinction more robustly, though some dialects show deretroflexion.
Historical simplification where retroflex clusters fronted to dental or alveolar geminates.
Ongoing de-retroflexion in younger speakers who neutralize the contrast between alveolar and retroflex stops.

sources: Masica, C. P. (1991). The Indo-Aryan Languages. Cambridge University Press.
